title: What Is Regenerative Agriculture? The Real Answer (Not the Corporate Version)
metaDescription: Regenerative agriculture restores soil biology instead of depleting it. Scotty explains what it actually means, why it matters, and what Albert Howard knew a hundred years ago that we're just now catching up to.
content:
# What Is Regenerative Agriculture? The Real Answer (Not the Corporate Version)

I want to talk about regenerative agriculture because people are using that phrase a lot right now, and I think most of what gets said about it misses the point. You hear it from big food companies who are trying to put a green label on industrial practices. You hear it from policy people who define it in the most watered-down way possible. And then there's the version that actually matters—the one that has to do with living soil and the biology of the earth, which is what I think about every single day I'm out in my garden.

So let me give you the honest version.

## What Regenerative Agriculture Actually Means

Regenerative agriculture is a way of growing food that rebuilds the health of the soil instead of depleting it. That's the whole thing. Everything else—the no-till, the cover crops, the rotational grazing, the composting—all of those are just different expressions of one central idea: soil is a living community, and your job as a grower is to support that community rather than mine it.

The formal definition you'll find in the academic literature is something like: a holistic approach to farming and land management that seeks to restore and enhance the health of soil, water, and other natural resources. The four core principles that show up consistently across research are: enhancing soil health, optimizing resource management, reducing climate impact, and improving biodiversity.

But I'm not a science guy. I'm a guy who watches what happens when he stops adding synthetic fertilizer to his raised beds and starts feeding the soil with compost instead. And what happens is remarkable. The plants get healthier. The food tastes better. The biology in the ground starts working for you instead of against you.

## Albert Howard Figured This Out Before Your Grandfather Was Born

Here's what I think is the most important thing to understand about regenerative agriculture: it is not a new idea. Albert Howard was writing about this in the 1940s. His book *An Agricultural Testament*, published in 1940, is one of the most important documents in the history of agriculture, and most people have never heard of it.

Howard spent decades in India studying traditional farming practices and came back with a fundamental insight: the fertility of healthy soil depends on the decomposition of organic matter by living organisms. He called this the Law of Return—everything that comes from the earth must go back to the earth. The decay cycle is not waste management. It is the engine of productivity.

What industrial agriculture did was sever that cycle. Instead of feeding soil biology with organic matter, it started feeding plants directly with synthetic chemical inputs—nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium in forms that the plant could absorb without the soil community being involved. It worked, in the short term. Yields went up. But the soil biology died. And once the biology dies, you need more and more chemical input just to maintain the same output. That's the treadmill modern agriculture is stuck on.

Regenerative agriculture is the project of getting off that treadmill.

## What the Decay Cycle Actually Is

I make a video every so often where I walk people through my decay cycle, because I think it's the most important thing I do. On one side of my backyard, there are the raised beds—that's the growth cycle, the production side. On the other side, there's a fifty-gallon drum that my friend's restaurant kitchen fills with organic scraps: onion skins, carrot greens, vegetable trimmings. I layer those with wood chips I get from local tree services. That pile heats up, breaks down, and eventually becomes the compost that feeds the beds.

That is regenerative agriculture at the backyard scale. Every bit of organic matter that comes through the system goes back into the system. Nothing leaves as waste. The fertility of the soil increases over time rather than declining.

Gabe Brown documents what this looks like at the farm scale in *Dirt to Soil*. His ranch in North Dakota went from conventional grain farming to a no-till, diverse cover-cropped, rotationally grazed operation over about twenty years. His soil organic matter went up. His input costs went down. His yields matched or exceeded his conventional neighbors. The soil got better every year instead of requiring more chemical intervention to stay productive.

## What the Science Says

For anyone looking at this from an academic angle—the UPSC angle, the policy angle—here's the research foundation. Peer-reviewed studies show that regenerative practices consistently increase soil organic matter, which improves water retention, carbon sequestration, and nutrient cycling. Healthy soil with higher organic matter absorbs and stores water more effectively, which reduces erosion and runoff.

The biodiversity component is significant too. Living soil supports bacterial communities, fungal networks, protozoa, nematodes, earthworms—a whole food web that functions as the plant's nutritional delivery system. When you disrupt that community with tillage or synthetic inputs, you force the plant to survive on whatever you apply from the outside. When you support it, the community delivers nutrients to the root system in bioavailable forms, continuously, as the plant needs them.

This is why regenerative agriculture is showing up in climate policy conversations. Rebuilding soil organic matter is one of the most scalable carbon sequestration strategies available. It doesn't require new technology—it requires changing practices that we know how to change.

## The Problem With How the Term Gets Used

Here's my issue with the corporate adoption of this language. Regenerative agriculture is being slapped on supply chains that still use synthetic fertilizer, still till, and still operate at industrial scale with no meaningful soil improvement occurring. When Kellogg's or General Mills announces a regenerative agriculture program, what they usually mean is incremental reductions in a few inputs on a small percentage of their supplier farms. That is not regenerative. That is marketing.

Real regenerative agriculture requires an honest relationship with the biology of the soil. It requires patience, because the soil takes time to recover. It requires accepting that you can't measure soil health on a quarterly earnings call. It requires thinking in decades rather than seasons.

The growers doing real regenerative work—the small farm operators, the backyard producers, the ranchers running true rotational grazing on diverse pasture—they don't have the marketing budget to compete with the corporate version. But their soil is getting better every year. That matters more than the label.

## Sources

- [What is Regenerative Agriculture? - Noble Research Institute](https://www.noble.org/regenerative-agriculture/)
- [The Fundamental Principles of Regenerative Agriculture and Soil Health - Noble Research Institute](https://www.noble.org/regenerative-agriculture/soil/the-fundamental-principles-of-regenerative-agriculture-and-soil-health/)
- [Regenerative Agriculture - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regenerative_agriculture)
- [Importance of regenerative agriculture: climate, soil health, biodiversity - Springer Nature](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43621-024-00662-z)
- [Regenerative Agriculture 101 - CSU Chico Center for Regenerative Agriculture](https://www.csuchico.edu/regenerativeagriculture/ra101-section/index.shtml)
